title: Pega Reporting & Analytics — Complete Deep Dive
content:
# Pega Reporting & Analytics — Complete Deep Dive

## Reporting Overview
Pega provides a comprehensive reporting and analytics framework that enables business users, managers, and administrators to monitor operations, track KPIs, and make data-driven decisions. Reports in Pega query the underlying database and present results in tables, charts, and dashboards.

## Report Types

### Report Definition
The primary reporting tool in Pega — a configurable, no-code report builder:

#### Components
- **Class**: Which data to query (e.g., Work- class for cases, Assign- class for assignments)
- **Columns**: Properties to display (mapped to database columns)
- **Filters**: Conditions to narrow results (date range, status, operator, custom criteria)
- **Grouping**: Group results by a property (e.g., group by status, by team, by month)
- **Aggregation**: SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, MAX on numeric properties
- **Sorting**: Order results by one or more columns
- **Pagination**: Control how many rows per page

#### Report Definition Types
- **List report**: Tabular list of records (e.g., all open cases)
- **Summary report**: Aggregated data with grouping (e.g., case count by status)
- **Chart report**: Visual charts — bar, line, pie, scatter, stacked bar
- **Drillable reports**: Click on a summary row to see the detail records behind it
- **Subreport**: Embed one report inside another for composite views

#### Creating a Report Definition
1. Open App Studio → Reports → Create Report
2. Select the class to report on
3. Add columns (properties)
4. Add filters (date range, status, etc.)
5. Configure grouping and aggregation
6. Choose display format (list, summary, chart)
7. Preview and save

### List View
A simplified table view associated with a case type:
- Configured in App Studio on each case type
- Displays in the portal as the default view when users browse cases
- Supports inline actions (open, reassign, bulk actions)
- Columns are configurable per case type
- Supports filtering, sorting, and search

### Dashboard
A page that combines multiple reports and widgets:
- **Slots**: Configurable panels arranged in a layout (2-column, 3-column, custom)
- **Widgets**: Each slot contains a report, chart, or custom widget
- **Personalization**: Users can customize their dashboard (add/remove/rearrange widgets)
- **Role-based**: Different dashboards for different roles (agent dashboard, supervisor dashboard, executive dashboard)
- **Real-time**: Dashboards can auto-refresh at configurable intervals

## Advanced Reporting Features

### Parameterized Reports
- Define parameters that users can set at runtime (date range, team, status)
- Parameters drive the report filters dynamically
- Used for reusable, multi-purpose report definitions

### Optimized Queries
- Pega generates SQL from report definitions
- **Report optimization**: Review generated SQL for performance. Add database indexes on frequently filtered/sorted columns.
- **Reporting database**: Configure a read replica for reporting queries to avoid impacting the primary database.
- **Pega Search (Elasticsearch)**: Use full-text search for text-heavy queries instead of database reports.

### Report Categories
- **Work reports**: Cases, assignments, SLAs, work queues
- **Process reports**: Flow execution, step duration, bottleneck analysis
- **Performance reports**: System performance, agent productivity, SLA compliance
- **Compliance reports**: Audit trail, security events, data changes
- **Custom reports**: Application-specific reports built for business needs

### Scheduled Reports
- Run reports on a schedule (daily, weekly, monthly)
- Output to email, file (CSV/Excel), or dashboard
- Configure via Admin Studio → Reports → Schedule
- Use for automated management reporting and compliance reporting

## Pega Insights & Analytics

### Pega Workforce Intelligence
- Track agent productivity metrics (handle time, resolution rate, idle time)
- Identify training needs based on performance gaps
- Compare team and individual performance
- Recommend process improvements

### Pega Process Mining
- Analyze actual process execution data to identify bottlenecks
- Compare actual flow to designed flow — find deviations
- Quantify the cost of rework loops and delays
- Suggest process optimizations using AI

### Real-Time Event Processing
- Monitor business events in real-time
- Trigger alerts when thresholds are breached (e.g., SLA violations, queue size)
- Stream processing for high-volume event data

## Database & Performance Considerations

### Reporting Database
- For high-volume systems, configure a read replica (reporting database) separate from the production database
- Route report queries to the read replica to avoid impacting case processing
- Configure in Admin Studio → Data Management → Database
- Slight data delay (replication lag) but prevents reporting from slowing down production

### Index Optimization
- Identify slow reports using PEGA0002 alerts (long-running queries)
- Add database indexes on columns used in filters and sorting
- Common columns to index: pxCreateDateTime, pxUrgencyAssign, pyStatusWork, pxAssignedOperatorID
- Work with your DBA to analyze query plans and add appropriate indexes

### Report Performance Best Practices
- **Filter early**: Add specific filters to reduce the result set before aggregation
- **Limit columns**: Only include columns you need — wide queries are slower
- **Avoid SELECT * patterns**: Be explicit about which properties to include
- **Use summary reports**: Aggregated reports are faster than listing every record
- **Paginate**: Don't load thousands of rows at once — use pagination
- **Cache results**: For expensive reports that don't change frequently, cache the results
- **Schedule off-hours**: Run expensive reports during low-usage periods

## Troubleshooting

**Report returns no data**
- Check filters — are they too restrictive? Remove filters one by one to identify the issue.
- Check class — are you querying the right class? Work- vs History- vs Assign-.
- Check access — row-level security may be filtering out records the user can't see.
- Check database — is the data actually in the table? Use direct SQL to verify.

**Report is slow (PEGA0002)**
- Review the generated SQL in the report's Advanced tab or Tracer.
- Check for missing database indexes on filtered/sorted columns.
- Reduce the date range or add more specific filters.
- Consider using the reporting database instead of the production database.
- Check for table scans — large tables without appropriate indexes cause full scans.

**Chart not rendering**
- Verify the report returns numeric data for the chart axis.
- Check grouping configuration — charts need at least one group column.
- Clear browser cache — chart rendering libraries may be cached.
- Check for JavaScript errors in browser console.

**Dashboard widget shows error**
- Verify the report underlying the widget exists and runs successfully standalone.
- Check the widget configuration — correct report reference, correct parameters.
- Check the user's access to the report and the data it queries.

## Interview & Certification Q&A

**Q: What is the difference between a Report Definition and a List View?**
A: A Report Definition is a general-purpose, configurable report that can query any class and produce lists, summaries, or charts. A List View is a simplified, case-type-specific table view that appears as the default browse view in portals. List Views are quick to set up but less flexible than Report Definitions.

**Q: How do you optimize a slow report in Pega?**
A: Steps: (1) Review the generated SQL to identify the bottleneck. (2) Add database indexes on columns used in WHERE, ORDER BY, and GROUP BY clauses. (3) Add more specific filters to reduce the result set. (4) Use a reporting database to avoid impacting production. (5) Consider summary reports instead of detail lists. (6) Schedule expensive reports for off-peak hours.

**Q: How does row-level security affect reports?**
A: Pega automatically appends security filters to report queries based on the user's Access of Role to Object (ARO) configuration and reporting filters. This means users only see data they're authorized to access. If a report returns no data for a user, row-level security filters may be excluding all records.

**Q: What is the reporting database and why is it important?**
A: The reporting database is a read replica of the production database used exclusively for report queries. It prevents expensive reporting queries from competing with case processing transactions for database resources. There's a slight delay (replication lag) but it ensures production performance isn't degraded by reporting activity.
